speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five of an interview with Danka Cyngler. At the end of the last tape, I was just asking you if there was any message or lesson that you would like to pass on to future generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had in my heart, always, that I like to give my children the best what can be for their future, which nobody can take away. This is study. Because this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can-- nobody can take them away. And that's so important to be knowledgeable. And I was very lack of it. I didn't have it. I still, today, am ready to study, even I'm old. I love to listen, to hear, to read. Just it's too late. For them, didn't cost-- didn't worry me nothing, just the kids to go the right way and the honest way. I did send them to an ordinary school, to a public school. I did want them to mix with all children. I want them to have knowledge that everybody is good. Just you know that you're Jewish, and you try to do what you should-- only love everybody. Because they are all the same people. We are all coming from the same places. We're going back to this same earth. And we should like each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: We shouldn't hate. Because there is not no reason to hate. We dying anyway. Why to be killed so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told the kids to have open eyes and ears. Because we don't know what can happen. Not to be a sheep, to be ready for everything, to learn, to coach, to study, to fight, to be able. Somebody knock you, give him back. Don't do like a sheep. We've been sheeps for you. Even we try to save your life. So I hope that never will come. That's all what I could tell them. And the rest I already [INAUDIBLE]. And I know that that is true. Only the grandchildren are going different direction. Their life is now very bad. It cannot be worse what is happen now, the drugs, with the killing and this all. This doesn't work so nice. I hope that will be stopping, and we will have good future generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I survived. It was-- I wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. That was supposed to be like that. I don't know if that's God or not. I don't want to say that doesn't exist. Life gives everybody opportunity, and-- but I don't know. I don't want to touch religion. Because there is too many people believe in that. And that's a good direction to go. Just everybody should respect each other. Because we all the same people, we're nothing different. Everybody wakes up, needs a wash, needs a drink, needs the same. We cannot do otherwise. We should respect each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to add that I can never forgive these people. I forgive-- I can't forget what happened to my beautiful family. [SOBS] And I very sorry what happened that shouldn't happen. That's all what I can say. And I hope this never will be again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In life, you're not supposed to be lazy. You have to work when you want something, not to get from nothing. You have to work. I worked all my life. I had three kids, and I worked without stop. I took home work. I, I-- I didn't know what this means, not to work. Today I'm older. So I do a bit less. Only first time change. People were more educated. They looking different work. For me, doesn't matter. I just study the overlook, shop keeper. I was working everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for telling your story. Thank you. Can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daddy, my mommy, and my younger sister, [NON-ENGLISH], Tova.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, I met him in Israel, in coincidence. He was keeping this in pocket, from my parents. Because he used to live in Poland in 1939, '40, they all came to us, the brothers of my daddy. And one of his brother, [NON-ENGLISH], had this photo. And he went through the war in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. So you start from the left, yeah. Sorry, can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's my father's brothers. One is [NON-ENGLISH]. That's my father in the middle, and Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940 or '39, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all come to us because they run away from Poland, because the German occupied them. So that was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, [NON-ENGLISH] had them with him. He give me when I met him in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, thank you, OK. Can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is my mother's sister, the older sister. She was to us like a Nana. Because my mother was born the same day what her daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hudia. And I didn't know my Nana, my mother's mother. She passed away. Only her daughter and my mother was in one age. So she was to me like a Nana.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't know. Because I got this from Argentina. She sent to her sister, used to live in Argentina. She passed away. Only her kids are alive. There is four children in Argentina. I've got 40 people living in Argentina, my family. So the auntie who just sent to Auntie Rosa a photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you. Can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This a nun which used to look after the kids. Her name is Santa Teresa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- when was this photograph--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was made in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got it from my friends in Israel. That's how I got it, from my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is Mother Superior. She was a nun. In 1941, she had to take off her habit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how did you obtain this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my girlfriends, which lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Can you please tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is supposed to be all our kids and Ukrainian really children, and a few of the Jews girls what was hidden. They, the Ukrainian didn't know. The girls didn't know they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, OK, thank you. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our marriage. I, my husband, my young sister, and friends, which we knew them from Tel Aviv and from kibbutz. Two girls are from kibbutz, and the two boys are Henry's friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 Of March, in [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a place where we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my Auntie Rosa, Mom's sister, and her husband and her kids. Some of them are married, their husbands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be before the war. And where was it-- In Argentina. I got them from my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Can you please tell me who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my children, my family. And also, this 10 years ago, we celebrate 40 years marriage, I and my husband. So my sister came with her husband. And we celebrate together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband and my grandson, which had bar mitzvah two years ago, and my son, my older son, Charlie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sons, two daughter-in-laws, and three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago. It was a bar mitzvah of Elliot. There is Sarah, Claire, Elliot, Jim, Jack, Karen, Lois.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two grandchildren, Richie, 21, and Jessica 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
